title: Cedar Mailbox Post with Corbels
summary: A cedar mailbox post with decorative corbels, joined with half-laps and biscuits and finished with boiled linseed oil.
description:
A basic mailbox post is a 4x4 in the ground with a box screwed to the top; this one adds a pair of corbels under the mailbox arm, which turns a purely functional post into something that reads as a considered piece of yard furniture.

Cedar is the right species here for two reasons at once: it resists rot without needing pressure treatment, and it's noticeably lighter to set in the ground than a pressure-treated post of the same size.

The finish is **boiled linseed oil, not paint or stain.** Oil soaks into the cedar rather than sitting on top of it, which lets the wood weather naturally over the years instead of needing a repaint when a film finish eventually cracks or peels outdoors.

tools.note: Cuts the half-lap joints where posts and corbels meet.
Routs the 1/2in cove profile on the corbel edges.

materials.name: Cedar, 4x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Main post — allow extra length to cut past any drying checks.

materials.name: Mineral spirits
materials.note: Thins the linseed oil for finishing.

cutList.note: Cut extra length past any drying checks at the ends.

steps.title: Cut the post and corbel blanks
steps.body: Cut the main post 96" from the 10 ft 4x4, trimming past any drying checks at the ends. Four 18" corbel blanks plus their saw kerfs come to a little over 72", so the 6 ft 4x4 yields only three — take the fourth from the 24" left over on the post board. Then cut the 24" mailbox arm from the 2x4, three 24" cap boards from the 2x6s and the 24" trim cap from the 1x6; every one of those is the same 24", so they can all be batched off one stop setting.

steps.title: Cut the half-lap joints
steps.body: A half-lap in 3-1/2" stock means taking 1-3/4" of depth out of each of the two mating pieces, so their faces come flush when the joint closes. Mark the width of the lap off the actual mating part rather than off a tape, set the blade to 1-3/4", and nibble the waste out in repeated passes. Cut one joint and test it before running the rest — cedar is soft, and a lap cut a hair deep will never close tight. Dry-fit every corbel to the post before any glue leaves the bottle; the plan does not fix where on the post each corbel lands, so that position is yours to set now, not after glue-up.

steps.title: Rout the corbel profile
steps.body: Rout the 1/2" cove along the decorative edges of each corbel in two shallow passes rather than one full-depth cut — cedar tears out badly at the exit if you rush it. Do this while each blank is still a loose piece you can clamp flat to the bench; routing an assembled corbel means routing around the post. Stop the cove short of the half-lap shoulders so the joint still closes on clean, square faces.

steps.title: Glue and reinforce the joints
steps.body: Glue the half-laps with Titebond III — it is the waterproof one in that family and the only sensible choice for a joint that lives outdoors for years. Reinforce each lap with #20 biscuits; the plan supplies twelve for four corbels, so that is three per joint. Clamp across the lap with bar clamps, confirm the corbel is square to the post before the glue skins over, and leave it clamped until fully cured rather than until it feels firm.

steps.title: Assemble the post and mount
steps.body: Screw the 24" 2x4 arm across the top of the post with 3" deck screws, then lay the three 24" 2x6 cap boards side by side to make a 16-1/2" x 24" platform for the mailbox, and finish with the 24" 1x6 trim cap. Predrill every screw — cedar splits readily near an end, and a split at the top of the post is permanent. The plan states neither the stacking order of the cap layers nor how deep the post is set, so dry-assemble the whole top before committing, and set the post depth to your local frost line and to whatever mailbox height your postal service requires.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: The plan's 16 oz of boiled linseed oil to 8 oz of mineral spirits is a two-to-one mix; use the thinnest cut for the first coat so it soaks deep into the cedar, then follow with one or two coats closer to straight oil. Wipe off everything that has not been absorbed after fifteen minutes or so — oil left standing on the surface stays tacky and never really cures. Oil-soaked rags generate heat as they dry and are a genuine fire hazard: spread them flat outdoors or drown them in water before they go in a bin.
